coredata.dc:title: High quantum yield photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots for switch sensing and imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yi, Zhihui
Li, Ximing
Zhang, Hongyu
Ji, Xiuling
Sun, Wei
Yu, Yuexin
Liu, Yinan
Huang, Jiaxing
Sarshar, Zahra
Sain, Mohini
coredata.dc:description: Stable blue fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with a very high quantum yield up to 81% has been reported for the first time. Novel N-CDs were synthesized through an efficient and rapid one-step hydrothermal synthesis process from diethylenetriamine as nitrogen source and a novel carbon source trans-aconitic acid. The nanosized particles of N-CDs were in the range of 2–8 nm and uniformly distributed in molecular level. The N-CDs showed high selectivity toward Fe3+ with low detection limit of 10.42 nmol L−1 (with corresponding linear range of 2–50 μmol L−1) enabling them for ion detection application and also exhibited high fluorescence stability in extreme pH conditions. Novel N-CDs also presented a green emission shift under acidic condition (pH~2) which makes them a potential sensing probe for security papers, food packaging and bio-medical detection sensors. A security paper sensor device has been fabricated and its operation function has been validated by making real time detection of color. The novel and facile to manufacture carbon dots has potential applications ranging from biological nano-sensors for security document to color-switch sensing and bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Iron sensing
Photoluminescence
pH detection
Quantum yield
Imaging
Security ink

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of pH on emission of CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) and b) HRTEM images of as-prepared CDs; c) Raman spectra of CDs; (G band = 1600 cm−1, D band = 1350 cm−1); d) XRD Pattern of CDs; e) FTIR spectra of CDs.
a) UV–vis absorption, excitation and PL emission of as-prepared CDs; b) emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelength (305–405 nm).

a) Change on PL intensity at 435 nm of CDs in presence of different metal ions; b) Ion concentration-dependent PL intensity of CDs with presence of Fe3+; c) Stern-Volmer plot of the quenching of fluorescence of N-CDs (Change on PL intensity versus concentration of Fe3+); d) FTIR of CDs in presence of different amount of Fe3+.

a) Fe3+ ion solution in CDs under natural light; b) quenching effect of CDs for reversible Fe3+ detection under UV light; c) schematic mechanism of reversible metal ion detection; d) Fe3+ ions solution in CDs under UV light after 10 cycles.

a) Photographs of CDs in acidic and basic aqueous solutions; b) reversible pH detection mechanism; c) photographs of CDs in aqueous solution with diverse pH values under 350 nm UV light illumination; d) PL spectra of CDs dispersed in solution with pH varying from 1 to 4.
Demonstrations of N-CDs as a security paper detector in three states. 1) paper without drop-casting; 2) paper with drop-casted CDs and 3) paper with drop-casted and acid written.
The proposed mechanism of CDs formation.
a) Effects of reaction temperature (a), reaction time (b), molar ratio of carbon and nitrogen sources (c) and reaction pH (d) on PLQY.

row.entry: Molar ratio between carbon and nitrogen sources

head.ce:title: High quantum yield photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots for switch sensing and imaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: John H. Daniels Faculty of Architecture, Landscape, and Design

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Stable blue fluorescent nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with a very high quantum yield up to 81% has been reported for the first time. Novel N-CDs were synthesized through an efficient and rapid one-step hydrothermal synthesis process from diethylenetriamine as nitrogen source and a novel carbon source trans-aconitic acid. The nanosized particles of N-CDs were in the range of 2–8 nm and uniformly distributed in molecular level. The N-CDs showed high selectivity toward Fe3+ with low detection limit of 10.42 nmol L−1 (with corresponding linear range of 2–50 μmol L−1) enabling them for ion detection application and also exhibited high fluorescence stability in extreme pH conditions. Novel N-CDs also presented a green emission shift under acidic condition (pH~2) which makes them a potential sensing probe for security papers, food packaging and bio-medical detection sensors. A security paper sensor device has been fabricated and its operation function has been validated by making real time detection of color. The novel and facile to manufacture carbon dots has potential applications ranging from biological nano-sensors for security document to color-switch sensing and bio-imaging.

The nitrogen-doped carbon dots synthesized from diethylenetriamine and trans-aconitic acid with a very high quantum yield of 81% provided high photoluminescence properties and good sensitivity and selectivity for Fe3+ detection. They also presented color switching in high acidic media enabling them to be used as pH sensor and security ink.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), carbon-based nanomaterials with size below 10 nm [1], have drawn increasing attention in the last decade. Superior properties including high photoluminescence (PL), high biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, high chemical stability, good water solubility and high productivity with low cost make CDs strong candidates for extensive applications in biomedical, electrical and optical fields. Promising uses of CDs in bioimaging [2], photothermal therapy [3], photocatalysis and organic light emitting diodes [4] have been reported. CDs are either synthesized using top down approaches [5–7], or bottom up carbonization from organic molecules [8–12]. Bottom up methods are more efficient for synthesis of highly fluorescent CDs in large scale. Carbon sources with abundant carboxylic groups are favorable ones in hydrothermal and pyrolysis methods [13–16]. Various doping routes were also developed to improve fluorescence properties and variability of CDs. N-doping using primary amines [17–19] is the most common and effective one in single doping strategies. Co-doping using nitrogen with another element including oxygen, sulphur [20] and phosphorus [21] in synthesis of high photoluminescence CDs were reported.

New synthetic strategies of CDs with higher QYs and their application have been reported in a large amount. However, it is still difficult to conjecture any specific chemical structures or exact CDs photoluminescence center. Previous studies indicate that the PL effects of CDs originate from both surface defects created by functional groups and intrinsic state of the inner carbon core [1]. Surface defects on CDs result from the surface passivation of starting materials. A direct relationship between the amount of CO groups and QY of CDs was proposed [22]. Ding et al. reported that the gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) was narrowed with increasing surface oxidization, allowing decrease in PL energy to obtain emission with higher wavelength [23]. Their idea was confirmed by an inverse experiment in which the emission wavelength of CDs was shifted from 520 nm (green) to 450 nm (blue) through a reduction pathway [24]. Despite the fact that the PL emission of CDs is significantly improved with nitrogen-doping, the doping and enhancement mechanism still remains unclear. Sun et al. [25] discussed a possible explanation that nitrogen atoms insert the graphene lattice in CDs through the alkylation between N-containing dopants and oxygen-containing functional groups (ketone, alcohol, carboxylic groups). Pyridinic and pyrolic nitrogen atoms lead to formation of defects and more polyaromatic structure in a carbon core to enhance emission [26]. A positive correlation can be observed between the number of aromatic nitrogen and QY of CDs [27]. Another possible explanation is that nitrogen atom may serve as a electron-catalyst for surface passivation of CDs to improve their fluorescent properties [28]. Intrinsic state emission is mainly assigned to the quantum size effect of the inner carbon core. The CDs synthesized by Li et al., 1.2–3.8 nm in size, obtained an obvious red-shift from UV light to near-infrared with a narrower HOMO-LUMO gap in experienced emission as a result of an increase in particle sizes of CDs [7]. However, an inverse trend was observed in CDs synthesized by Kwon and Rhee due to the presence of oleylamine ligands, which reduces the energy gap by donating lone pairs to the LUMOs of the inner sp2 clusters [29]. The diverse correlation between CDs size and emission color was supported with a theoretical model. For CDs with graphitized core, there is a negative relationship between CD size and PL energy; while for CDs with amorphous core, the PL energy increases with larger sizes [30]. Besides the mainstream view, a temperature dependent synthetic mechanism suggested that the fluorescence of CDs originates from organic fluorophores connecting to the surface of CDs, formed by dehydration between the carbon and nitrogen sources at low reaction temperature [15,31]. At higher temperature, fluorophores are further dehydrated to form the carbon core leading a lower emission but higher photostability. Generally, a well proposed clarification of PL mechanism, especially for heteroatom doping is highly significant for future synthesis and application of this carbon nano-material.

Since iron known as a vital element found in most of the physiological processes such as oxygen uptake, oxygen metabolism, electron transfer and enzymatic catalysis, its excess or deficiency will cause damages to the live systems [32]. Therefore, it is of great importance to detect iron ions with high sensitive and selective materials like fluorescent sensors. Carbon dots are known as one of the most widely used sensors with high sensitivity and photoluminescence properties for Fe3+ detection. In addition to be detectable in response to some metal ions (Fe, Ag, and Hg) [33], the CDs are known to be used a pH fluorescent sensing probe in the environmental and biological applications due to their rich functional groups on their surface. The pH detection for instance, is essential for evaluating the quality of drinking water which has a variation in pH due to different types of ions contained in the soil of different water sources [34].

In this study, trans-aconitic acid (TAA), an organic acid that is naturally present in human urine, is used as a novel carbon source along with a diethylenetriamine (DETA) as a nitrogen source to synthesize CDs with QY as high as 81%. The reaction conditions influencing the fluorescence properties were studied by altering reaction time, temperature, pH, molar ratio between carbon and nitrogen sources. Different characterization techniques were carried out on as-prepared CDs to view their surface chemistry and internal structure as well as optical properties. The as-prepared CDs were used in pH and metal ion (particularly Fe3+) detections and possible mechanisms related to the functional groups and ion interaction were suggested.

Trans-aconitic acid (TAA), diethylenetriamine (DETA), sodium hydroxide, sulphuric acid and metal salts for ion detection were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All resources were directly used in experiments without any modification or further purification. Distilled water was used as solvent for all trials and characterization.

The fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using a hydrothermal synthesis method. 0.52 g (3 mmol) TAA and 540–2695 μl (9–15 mmol) DETA were dissolved in 15 ml distilled water (DW) with slight stirring to obtain a homogeneous solution. pH was adjusted to 2.0–11.0 using solid sodium hydroxide and 1 mol L−1 sulphuric acid. The mixture was then transferred and sealed into a 20 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in oven at 120–200 °C for 2–6 h. After reaction, the solution was cooled to room temperature naturally. The dark yellowish solution was dialyzed against distilled water through a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000 Da) for 12 h to remove excess precursors not participating in the reaction. The resultant was filtered with a 0.22 nm filter paper. Final yellow products were placed in ultrasonication bath in order to achieve an even dispersion of CDs in solution.
High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of CDs were obtained with H–7650B electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan).

A Philips X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) machine was used with a PW 1830 HT generator, a PW 1050 goniometer, and PW3710 control electronics. The powdered XRD patterns were recorded on a zero-reflective silicon substrate.

Raman SENTERRA spectrometer was used to characterize the graphite and disordered structure in the samples. The spectrometer used a laser of 2 mW, a wavelength of 534 nm and a confocal OLYMPUS BX51 optical microscope, with 20× metallurgical objective. The spectral range was 1000–2000 cm−1, the resolution was 3–5 cm−1 and the integration time and co-addition were set to 10 s and 5, respectively.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CD were measured by a Bruker Tensor 27 IR with a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1 within the range of 4000 to 500 cm−1 using potassium bromide pellet as background.

The UV–vis spectra of the samples were acquired with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Evolution 60S UV–vis spectrophotometer, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.). The fluorescence spectra were detected using a LS-55 Fluorescence Spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Fremont, CA, USA).

The absolute quantum yield of carbon dots were determined using a previously reported integrating sphere method [35]. Samples were excited in an integrating sphere (Gigahertz Optik, custom made) with light from a 365 nm LED (Thorlabs M365L2), and photoluminescence was collected by a 1 mm diameter optical fiber (Ocean Optics) and detected by an Ocean Optics Maya 2000 spectrometer. The absolute quantum yield was calculated by taking appropriate ratios of the excitation and emission peak areas of spectra recorded with 1) the empty sphere, 2) the excitation focused directly onto the sample, and 3) the excitation focused onto the wall of the sphere, indirectly exciting the sample. The accuracy of the system was verified against literature values for rhodamine 6G and MEH-PPV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH on emission of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 60 μl of CDs sample was mixed thoroughly in 1 ml of distilled water. 10 mol L−1 sodium hydroxide and 10 mol L−1 sulphuric acid solutions were gradually added to the mixture to obtain solutions with pH range from 1 to 13.

Ion salts (Fe(NO3)3)·9H2O, CaCl2, and CuBr2, Al2(SO4)3, KCl, MgCl2, LiCl and NaCl were dissolved in distilled water to obtain 0.6 μmol L−1 ion solutions. Various concentrations of ion solutions were prepared by adding different amount of 0.6 μmol L−1 ion solution into 3 ml water with 50 μl purified CDs solution to reach concentration gradient from 2 μmol L−1 to 50 μmol L−1. The PL intensity of the control group (50 μl CDs solution in 3 ml water) was measured and defined as F0. The PL intensity of CDs in ion solution (F1) was measured 1 min after mixing thoroughly to ensure complete interaction between the metal ions and CDs.

The inner morphology of CDs was characterized by HRTEM, XRD and Raman techniques while FTIR was used to identify surface chemistry. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, CDs have spherical shape with size of 2–8 nm and are uniformly distributed. The lattice size of CDs is 0.23 nm. The XRD pattern in Fig. 1d shows a broad hump around 2θ equal to 25° indicating an amorphous structure of carbon dots, however this position is referred to the presence of low-graphitic carbon structure corresponding to (002) diffraction. At the reaction temperature for CDs synthesis, a fully graphitized nanostructure could not be formed but the formation of graphitic clusters with ca. 5–8 aromatic rings is carried out [37]. The presence of these graphitic clusters in our CDs was confirmed by both HRTEM images and Raman spectra.

The Raman spectrum clearly presents strong G-band (1600 cm−1) for in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms and weak D-band (1350 cm−1) for defects in sp2 carbons (Fig. 1c). A large G to D intensity ratio (1.73 in our case) indicates the formation of a highly crystallized lattice structure for CDs carbon core which is consistent with the results from HRTEM. The absence of strong 2D band (around 2700 cm−1) demonstrates that the inner core might not be a single graphene layer structure [38]. As shown in Fig. 1e, the presence of abundant hydroxyl group is affirmed with strong O–H bending band at 3434 cm−1 due to the presence of –COOH group. A weaker peak at 3281 cm−1 originates from conjugated amine groups (stretching vibrations of N–H) connected to the carbon core. These functional groups result in N-CDs having good hydrophilic properties which provide good solubility for CDs. The stronger peaks at 1665-1583 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of the amide linkage indicating the incorporation of many desired amino groups in the N-doped CDs structure. These revealed CO stretching, O–H bending and N–H bending bands indicate the presence of carboxylic and amide functional groups on CDs surface. It is suggested that during the hydrothermal reaction, primary amines in nitrogen sources were carbonized into CDs and carboxylic acid groups on carbon sources were converted into amide groups. The peaks at 1490-1121 cm−1 are assigned to the CC, C–N, and C–O groups.

The UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of the N-CDs are presented in Fig. 2 in order to investigate the effect of surface passivation on their optical properties. The obtained CDs have two UV–Vis absorption bands, the peak centered at 230 nm originates from the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds in the carbon core and the other band located around 340 nm is assigned to absorption of connected carbonyl/amine functional groups (Fig. 2a). The excitation wavelength of CDs (365 nm) is relatively close to 340 nm, which demonstrates that the blue fluorescence might originate from functional groups attached to the carbon core. The emission spectra of CDs ranges from 350 nm (purple) to 550 nm (yellow). The emission peak centers at 435 nm with an excitation-dependent pattern in the excitation wavelength of 305–355 nm. The emission peak experienced a gradual left shift as the excitation wavelength increased from 365 to 405 nm. The shift, which might due to switch of fluorescent originated from various functional groups or a wide size distribution of carbon dots, is too slight for naked eyes to be observed and blue fluorescence is still maintained. The aqueous solution of the as-prepared CDs is transparent, clear under natural light, while it emits vivid blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light excitation (Fig. 2a, inset).

TAA was used as the carbon source and DETA as nitrogen source in this experiment. The absolute QY of CDs found to be up to 81%, significantly higher than that of some other N-CDs reported in the literature [39–42]. The QY of CDs was influenced by reaction time and temperature, molar ratio of carbon and nitrogen sources and reaction pH. To optimize the PLQY of products and further investigate the synthesis mechanism of CDs, these reaction conditions were controlled respectively in different trials. To explore the effect of temperature, same amount of reactants in distilled water were heated for 4 h in the oven at three different set of temperatures: 120 °C, 160 °C and 200 °C (Table 1a). It has been found that the QY of CDs increases at higher reaction temperature and it reaches its maximum at 200 °C (Table 1a). Similar procedures were done to vary reaction time to 2, 4 and 6 h and the optimized reaction time of 4 h resulting the PLQY of 76% (Table 1b) was attained. The increasing in reaction time and temperature provides a better environment for hydrothermal reaction and leads to a more complete attachment of DETA to TAA resulting more amide linkages connected to carbon core. As higher temperature induces the movement of particles in reactants, the longer reaction time simply provides more possibility of reactants interaction. However, over-heating may cause carbonization of amides into the inner carbon core structure resulting in decreased QY which might be the same scenario when the reaction time prolonged to 6 h.

The molar ratio of TAA to DETA was controlled in the range of 2:1 to 1:5 (2:1, 1:1, 1:3 and 1:5) (Table 1c). The results revealed that the increasing of nitrogen source (DETA) has a positive effect on QY of CDs as the highest PLQY of about 81% was attained with the molar ratio of 1:5. Amide groups formation was enhanced by increasing nitrogen atoms doped into CDs, allowing the increase in QY. Reaction pH of the solution was adjusted to 2.0, 5.0, 9.0 and 11.0 with other conditions remained unchanged (Table 1d). The optimized pH was found to be 9 resulting the PLQY of 77% which is very similar to the value obtained in the case of pH = 5 (PLQY = 76%). It was found that the reaction pH higher than 9 or lower than 5 resulted in a significant decrease in PLQY of CDs. When pH was lower than the pKa of TAA (3.15) or higher than pKa of DETA (10.45), the reactants were either protonated or deprotonated to their non-ionized states resulted in incomplete reactions. pH of 5 and 9 ionized both acid and amine sources, allowing better interaction between reactants and consequently resulting the highest PLQY among four pH values.

With strong fluorescence properties and high photostability of CDs, the applications of carbon dots in environmental and biomedical fields were vastly studied. Here, the CDs were used as fluorescence probes in detection of Fe ions. For this experiment, changes in fluorescence properties of N-CDs upon addition of various metal ion salts were measured (Fig. 3 ). The change in PL intensity (ΔF) of CDs at 435 nm in presence of different metal ions is presented in Fig. 3a. ΔF is the change in the photoluminescence property defined as F 0 –F where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensities at 435 nm in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. A significant fluorescent quenching was observed when 2 μl of Fe ions was introduced into CDs solution under the excitation of 435 nm while the addition of other metal ions resulted small (in the case of Cu, Al and Ca ions) or no obvious fluorescence changes (in the case of Na, Li, K and Mg ions). This result showed high selectivity of prepared CDs toward Fe ions compared to other metal ions. For a sensitivity study, different concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 1.0–50.0 μmol l−1 were investigated. Fig. 3b shows a gradual decrease in PL intensity at 435 nm with increasing Fe3+ concentration, indicating the ability of CDs to sense Fe3+ concentration. The fluorescence quenching data follows the Stern-Volmer equation [43]. The calculated quenching constant (Ksv) of CD solution in response to Fe3+ was found to be 0.024 × 104 L mol−1.
F 0
F
− 1 =
K
S V
c
where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, c is the analyte (Fe3+) concentration, and F0 and F are the PL intensities of CDs at 435 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively.

The Stern-Volmer plot in Fig. 3c shows an adequate linear fit with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.997 between the PL intensity and the concentration of Fe ions in the concentration range of 2–50 μl, which is below the maximum level of Fe ions in drinking water permitted by the US Environmental Protection Agency. The detection limit (DL) of 10.42 n mol L−1 was calculated according to the IUPAC 3ϭ criterion (DL = 3SD/S, where SD is the standard error of the intercept and S is the slope of the calibration curve) possessing one of the lowest detection limit among the reported values in the literature [44].

The potential interfering ions were also tested to check the selectivity of CDs toward metal ions. Common metal ions (Ca, Li, Al, In, K and Mg) were tested at the same concentration as Fe ions. The significant decay on PL intensity was only observed with Fe and Cu ions, while there was no obvious difference with other metal ions addition. The FTIR of CDs in presence of different amount of Fe3+ ions in Fig. 3d exhibited that the intensity of the functional groups corresponded to O–H, CO and N–H present on the surface of CDs decreased by increasing the Fe3+ ions concentration. This explains that these carboxyl and amide functional groups are responsible for iron detection by CDs. As the iron concentration increases, more of these functional groups will be involved in making bands with iron ions resulting in quenching of fluorescence of N-CDs in detection mechanism, thus their concentrations on the CDs surface and as a result their intensities in the FTIR spectra are decreased.

Iron detection is of great importance in many industrial and clinical applications as this transition metal plays an important role in organisms and their biological lives. Thus developing a practical Fe3+ selective luminescent nanosensor compatible with aqueous environments and/or resistant under high acidic conditions nowadays becomes a hot topic subject for many research studies. Modified passivated nanostructure carbon dots is one of these compatible nanosensor. The fluorescence of CDs is not caused by a single factor but arises from a combination of several factors such as size, surface passivation, functional groups, and heteroatoms [45].

As shown in Fig. 3a, we have investigated the PL responses of N-doped CDs in response to different types of cations. These results showed good fluorescent response and high selectivity of as-prepared N-CDs toward Fe3+ while other metal ions have no effect or only a slight effect on PL emission. Fig. 4 a and b shows multiple reversible cycles of Fe3+ detection under natural light and UV light, respectively. The strong blue emission of CDs under UV light is remarkably observable as well as their quenching effect while adding Fe3+ solution. The Fe3+ ion detection mechanism is presented in Fig. 4c. According to the FTIR data there are significant carboxylic/amide functional groups on the surface of the N-CDs. The quenching mechanism could be explained by the formation of coordination bonds between Fe3+ and the carboxylic/amide or phenolic hydroxyl groups of the CDs (Fig. 4c), however the luminescence mechanism for ion detection is still an open question. These complexes may alter the electronic structure of N-CDs and influence the distribution of excitons, which accelerate the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through effective photoelectron or energy transfer, further results in the fluorescence quenching [46,47]. The prepared N-CDs showed high photostability after 10 reversible cycles of iron detection (Fig. 4d) indicating good stability of the fluorescence signal and ensuring the accuracy of the detection results.

One of the application of using CDs for Fe3+ detection was reported by Gao et al. [48] who used the quenched CDs in the form of CDs/Fe3+ for imaging the cancerous cells. The cancer cells live in a reductive environment, accumulating higher level of GSH compared to normal cells. The CDs/Fe3+ units corporate with cells which lead to a higher level of GSH in cancerous cells by the achievement of tumor accumulation and exhibit observable fluorescence signals in the tumor site, hence cancer cells are differentiated from normal cells.

The emission of carbon dots demonstrated high stability in a wide pH range from 2.0 to 12.0. The PLQY higher than 50% was maintained in this pH range and the fluorescence behavior was easily observed with naked eyes. By adding acid to the solution of N-CDs, a visible green shift can be observed in lower pH range as demonstrated in Fig. 5 a. The fluorescence quenching due to the protonation and deprotonation of fluorescent functional groups could be seen in Fig. 5c. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs responses at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm for pH range of 1–4 is presented in Fig. 5d. It is evident that lower pH (pH 1 and 2) results in smaller emission wavelength which is expected through observed quenching effect of carbon dots in lower pH range giving hints for their potential applications in pH detection. The same trend of increase in the fluorescence intensity of carbon quantum dots with a rise of pH from 1.5 to 7 was reported by Zhou et al. [13]. The phenomena of increasing the PL intensity by increasing pH could be resulted from the different surface structures. With the pH increase, deprotonation of carboxylic groups is occurred on the surface of the CDs and a “protective shell” with negative charge is formed gradually on the surface of CDs with lower non-radiative recombination rate [49]. These results demonstrate that pH of the solution would affect the surface state of CDs and alter the band edge. The blue shift of PL emission wavelength with increase of pH is indeed as a result of surface defects modification caused by H+ and OH− concentration gradient. The different surface structure of carbon dots imposed either by surface modification or medium environment is the main cause of their different optical properties [50].

The reversible behavior of as-prepared N-doped carbon dots was observed upon switching between acid and base media (Fig. 5a). It is proposed that applying numerous acidic/basic cycles on N-CDs could imply the reversible protonation/de-protonation behavior of carboxylic/amide functional groups on the surface of the N-CDs (Fig. 5b). It is worthy to state that the as-prepared CDs showed strong stability in fluorescence activity with no damage in the range of pH 3–8 (Fig. 5c). Thus, considering common waste water conditions with low pH values, these CDs have potential applications in strongly acidic media.

Fig. 6 shows the sensing working mechanism using CDs and solvents (acid) security ink on a paper capable of high resolution detection. The first row shows a paper under visible light with drop cased CDs (number 2) as well as acid written drop cased CDs (number 3). Evidently, no letter was detected under visible light. In the second row the same papers in three states are exposed to UV light. The written letter CBBP was revealed with no light on acid pen written paper, thus making the paper easy to identify by using a selective solvent, which is a dilute sulphuric acid ink in our case.

The third row in Fig. 6 uses CDs as an ink to write on a UV exposed paper. Subsequently, the letters written with CDs were overwritten with an acid pen. Therefore, the blue CDs indicator in this case has been attacked by the acid. Once acid has been applied on the ink it showed no color for the part of the paper which was not drop-cased with CDs. Therefore, it demonstrates that alteration is fake.

The successful facile, low cost and rapid method for synthesizing highly efficient N-doped carbon dots through a hydrothermal method using diethylenetriamine as nitrogen source and a novel carbon source of trans-aconitic acid was established. The CDs showed strong blue fluorescence with a very high QY up to 81%. The FTIR confirmed the presence of high concentration of surface carboxyl/amide functional groups which in turn are responsible for different detection mechanism provided by CDs. The N-CDs were tested as a fluorescence probe for Fe3+ ion detection in aqueous solution. The selective high sensitivity of CDs to Fe3+ through mechanism of formation of coordination bonds between Fe3+ and the carboxylic/amide or phenolic hydroxyl groups of the CDs ensures their application in toxic ion detection. The obtained CDs also exhibited high fluorescence stability in extreme pH and experienced a green emission shift at highly acidic media with pH of 2, which demonstrated a potential role of CDs in pH detection. The PL intensity of CDs response to pH change showed increasing trend as pH of the solution increases due to deprotonation of the carboxylic groups. The N-doped CDs have also presented high photostability under repeated reversible cycles of Fe3+/PO4 3− addition as well as under acidic/basic media. The different optical behaviors of N-CDs arise from different surface defects present on their surface which affect the energy gap and surface state of these nanomaterials under surface modification or medium environment.

Zhihui Yi: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Ximing Li: Validation, Investigation, Resources, Hongyu Zhang: Methodology, Visualization, Xiuling Ji: Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Wei Sun: Validation, Investigation, Yuexin Yu: Validation, Investigation, Yinan Liu: Investigation, Resources, Jiaxing Huang: Validation, Resources, Methodology, Zahra Sarshar: Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Mohini Sain: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful for the financial support from Ontario Research Excellence Fund (ORF-RE), Ford Motor Canada and Total NA. Zhihui Yi and Ximing Li contribute equally in this paper. The authors also acknowledge Maria Semeniuk for her contribution to this paper.
